preFix.title: Stoneware iron oxide brushwork
preFix.subtitle: Bold iron oxide brush strokes on bisqueware sealed under a transparent glaze, fired to cone 6
preFix.excerpt: Brush an iron oxide wash in bold strokes onto bisque-fired stoneware, allow to dry, then apply a transparent or semi-matte overglaze. The iron darkens through the clear glaze during firing, producing tonal variation from amber to dark brown. Cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Handle raw iron oxide with gloves and mix wet only.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Iron oxide in raw powder form: wear gloves and a P100 respirator when measuring and mixing. Mix wet only. Never use raw iron oxide powder near food preparation. The pre-mixed wash is safer to use than dry powder; if mixing from powder, always add water first and add the oxide to the water rather than the reverse.
Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.
Iron oxide is one of the oldest ceramic colourants: at low concentration it fires warm amber; applied thickly it reaches dark brown to near-black. Brushed in bold strokes onto
bisque
ware and covered with a transparent or semi-matte overglaze, the iron becomes a tonal drawing sealed inside the glaze. After the
glaze firing
to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C), brush marks read as dark-on-light through the glaze surface. One thin coat gives amber-honey; three coats in the same area builds toward dark brown.

tools.name: Mop brush (for wide strokes and the overglaze coat)

materials.name: Iron oxide red or yellow, pre-mixed as a wash (10 g oxide to 100 ml water as a starting ratio)
Mid-fire transparent or semi-matte glaze, pre-mixed

content.text: Preparation

Mix the iron oxide wash: combine 10 g of iron oxide powder with 100 ml of water and stir thoroughly. This 10% concentration produces a mid-tone wash suitable for testing; reduce to 5 g per 100 ml for lighter ambers, increase to 20 g per 100 ml for dark, near-black marks. Always add the powder to the water with gloves and a respirator in place. Allow the mix to settle for a few minutes, then stir again immediately before use as the oxide settles quickly. Keep the jar lidded when not brushing. Wipe the bisqueware with a clean damp sponge and allow to dry for ten minutes before applying the wash.
Applying the iron oxide wash

Place the bisqueware on the banding wheel. Load the hake brush generously with the iron oxide wash and apply in bold, single-stroke passes across the surface. Do not scrub or go over wet marks: bisque is porous and pulls colour from the brush quickly. A single confident stroke in each area gives clean edges and tonal consistency.

Work with the form's natural geometry: vertical strokes on a cylinder, horizontal strokes on a wide bowl, diagonal strokes across a platter. The marks will be visible in the fired piece as the dark tonal design through the overglaze.

For tonal variation, apply two or three strokes in the same area on top of each other, allowing each to dry slightly between passes. The iron-rich area will build toward dark brown in the firing where multiple coats are applied.

Use the optional liner brush for fine lines, dots, or detail marks if the design requires them. Load the liner brush with a slightly thicker wash than the broad stroke brush: fine lines need more colour concentration to remain visible after the overglaze is applied.

Allow the iron oxide wash to dry completely. The surface should be uniform in sheen and the marks should look slightly darker than the bare bisque. A damp sponge can wipe away any unwanted marks before the wash is fully dry: correction is possible on bisque.
Applying the overglaze

Apply the transparent or semi-matte mid-fire overglaze over the dry iron oxide wash. Brush with the mop brush in three overlapping coats, allowing each to dry fully before the next, or dip the piece once in the glaze bucket. The glaze coat must be even: thin areas over the iron wash will fire with a rough, uneven texture.
Do not scrub the brush over the wash on the first overglaze coat: one quick stroke per area, no back-and-forth. The first stroke locks the oxide in place; later coats can be worked more freely.
Clean the foot-ring of any glaze before loading the kiln shelf. Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C).
Variations

Wiped-back wash: apply the iron oxide wash all over, allow to dry, then wipe back the surface with a lightly damp sponge, leaving colour only in recesses and texture. Apply the overglaze over the wiped surface.
Under a semi-matte glaze: the iron tones read more softly than under a full transparent glaze, giving a muted, earthy finish.
Layered with cobalt: paint iron oxide in some areas and cobalt blue in others, seal both under a transparent glaze, and fire for a two-colour surface.

rows.fix: Apply the first overglaze coat with single confident strokes in one direction only. On the second coat, multiple passes are fine as the first coat has locked the iron wash in place.
rows.cause: The overglaze brush was worked back and forth over the same area before the first stroke had dried into the bisque
rows.problem: Iron oxide lifted when the overglaze was applied

rows.fix: Increase the oxide concentration to at least 15 g per 100 ml, or apply two or three coats of the wash before the overglaze.
rows.cause: Iron oxide wash was too dilute, or only one thin coat was applied
rows.problem: Marks barely visible in the fired surface

rows.fix: Keep the wash concentration below 25 g per 100 ml for brushwork. Very dark marks need multiple thin coats rather than one heavy coat.
rows.cause: Iron oxide applied too thickly in some areas; the thick iron deposit did not fully melt into the glaze
rows.problem: Marks appear rough and raised under the overglaze after firing
